title: Role-Based Access Control: How to Build an RBAC App with AI
summary: YouTube video demonstrating how to build a role-based access control application using Softr's AI Co-Builder. The tutorial shows creating an event management system with roles, permissions, workflows, and automations without iterative prompting, comparing Softr against competitors like Lovable and Replit.
writeup: A sponsored walkthrough of Softr's AI Co-Builder that uses building an event management app (with roles, permissions, and automations) to argue a concrete case against pure vibe-coding platforms. The central claim: AI builders optimized for the first-five-minute demo quietly drain credits at the 20% polish stage, because they generate before asking about access logic, flatten relational data into string columns, and force re-prompts every time the schema changes. The proposed alternative is a hybrid model where AI scaffolds the structure, a visual editor handles roles and workflows, and a contained "Vibe Coding Block" handles only small bespoke UI logic.
key_points: Softr's Co-Builder asks about user groups and access rules before generating anything, so the permission model is correct the first time rather than requiring paid re-prompts to fix a wrong model built on the wrong assumptions.

Running the same one-sentence prompt on Lovable, Replit, and Softr revealed that Lovable produced a pretty UI where organizer vs. regular user was a label on a button rather than a real permission, while Replit scaffolded code but left the developer to write permission middleware, whereas Softr produced a properly normalized database with a sign-ups join table modeled as a real many-to-many relationship.

The Vibe Coding Block is a self-contained component dropped onto a page in one prompt for small custom UI logic (such as a live capacity counter that turns red below five spots), explicitly limited to that surgical role so it never touches the parts of the app that already work.

Workflow automations in Softr live next to the database schema, so when the schema changes the triggers update without regeneration; wiring the same trigger-send-confirm-notify-check-capacity flow through Zapier plus Make plus Postmark would require re-stitching the automation every time the schema shifts.

Softr's pricing model is a flat monthly fee covering database, app, workflows, and permissions, which the video contrasts with per-token or per-action credit meters on AI-native builders that stack charges before a single user touches the app.

The video draws a clear fit/no-fit boundary: Softr wins on business software with users, roles, data, and workflow (internal tools, portals, CRMs); consumer apps requiring custom animations, scroll-driven interactions, native gestures, or App Store distribution are explicitly outside its lane.
